re: Rainbow’s End is a bitter condemnation of colonisation.’ Discuss.

’A real home is where there are people looking out for each other’ How does
Rainbow’s End explore the importance of home?

An empty place for Papa Dear.' The men, in Harrison’s play are marginalised
and have little impact on the play’s events. To what extent do you agree?
The main hardships characters face in the play are caused by gender inequality. To what extent do you agree?
’The dream sequences in Rainbow’s End are the characters’ only escape from the depressing reality of life.’ Discuss.

Gladys and Dolly are torn between the world of white Australians and their
Indigenous Australian heritage.’ Discuss.
How does Harrison use setting to reflect characters’ experiences in Rainbow’s End?
To what extent does Rainbows End show that the characters optimism is justified?

We demand the right to control our own destiny.'
Rainbows End suggests that vulnerable people can control their own destinies.
Do you agree?

’Least here we do things our way-no one breathin’ down our necks.’
Home is central to identify in Rainbow’s End.
To what extend do you agree?

In Rainbow’s End, attempts to be helpful are often unsuccessful.
Discuss.
“But hessian! Like bandaid over a sore-” In Rainbow’s End, resolution is often an impossibility for marginalised individuals. To what extent to you agree?
How does Harrison convey the conflict that exists between conformity and identity?
“My ball gown? Courtesy of the town bloody tip?” Rainbow’s End presents the constant tension between society and self. Discuss.
How does Harrison convey a sense of hope in the face of adversity?
My girl, a graduate…” How does Rainbow’s End explore that people can be empowered despite their marginalised circumstances?
‘Families can be just as much a burden as a blessing.’ Discuss in relation to Rainbow’s End.
A loving family is all anyone needs.’ How does Rainbow’s End explore this idea?
“Your mum chops wood! With an axe and all!” How is the strength displayed throughout the play?
‘Hope is a powerful tool for change.’ To what extent does this apply to Rainbow’s End?
“Que sera sera.” Rainbow’s End argues that people should accept their fates and do not strive for better futures. To what extent do you agree?
“I don’t want my mother to be served last in the butcher’s.” How does Rainbow’s End explore how individuals can survive and overcome their experiences of discrimination?
‘Rainbow’s End suggests that family relationships can be destructive.’ To what extent do you agree?
‘Rainbow’s End tells a tragic tale about the loss of culture and identity.’ Towhat extent do you agree?
Rainbow’s End highlights the futility of hoping for a better future in a prejudiced world. To what extent do you agree?
‘Characters in Rainbow’s End are torn between the world of white Australians and their Indigenous Australian heritage.’ Discuss.

“I want what any mother, black, white or brindle, wants for her daughter.” 
Harrison presents mother-daughter relationships as complex. Discuss.
The main hardships characters face in the play are caused by gender inequality. To what extent do you agree?
Ultimately, Harrison presents an uplifting message about individual’s capacity to overcome any adversity. Discuss.
How does Harrison employ dramatic techniques to highlight the struggle and despair faced by Indigenous Australians?
How does Harrison utilise setting to demonstrate the hardships that Indigenous Australians encounter?
To what extent does Rainbow’s End show that optimism is justified in times of injustice?

“We demand the right to control our own destiny.” 
Rainbow’s End suggests that vulnerable people can control their own destinies. Do you agree?
The characters in Rainbow's End share similar experiences, but do not see things the same way. To what extent do you agree?
In Rainbow's End the women discover the power of having a voice. Discuss.

Relationships can be a catalyst to personal transformation and empowerment. 
How does Harrison explore the inherent influence of relationships in her play?
“Are you saying you’d rather live in a humpy by the river? When I’m promising you the world?” In Rainbow's End, attempts to be helpful are often unsuccessful. Discuss.
Oppression and other negative human experiences can have lasting impacts on an individual’s life and outlook. How is this idea explored in Rainbow’s End?
How does Rainbow’s End explore ideas about the ‘other’?
Ultimately, Jane Harrison’s play Rainbow’s End is a study in what it means to be human. Discuss.
‘Cultural connection is the greatest resistance in Rainbow’s End.’ Discuss.
In what ways does Harrison explore how the tensions between groups shapes individual and collective experiences?
How does Harrison use dramatic techniques to inspire audiences to sympathise with the characters in Rainbow’s End?
How does Harrison’s play explore the way ethnocentric Australia creates social and psychological experiences on individuals?
“It’ll be all right.” How does Harrison explore the resilience of Australian indigenous people in Rainbow’s End?
Explore the effectiveness of the ending of the play to position the audience to respond strongly to the themes or ideas presented by Harrison.
Harrison reveals that cultural values are significant in elucidating Aboriginal identity. How does she do this in Rainbow’s End?
“A real home is where there are people looking out for each other.” How does Rainbow’s End reveal the importance of home?
Social ostracization is presented by Harrison as a catalyst for power and change. To what extent do you agree with this statement?
Errol’s attempt to be “a knight in shining armour” highlights the complexity of being a man in a patriarchal society. Discuss.
Compare and contrast the character arcs of all the main characters in Rainbow’s End, highlighting their individual growth and the themes of resilience and self-discovery throughout the play.
Explore how voice within the play reflects the values and attitudes of the context within which it is set.
Discuss the stylistic choices made by Harrison to construct her play and to present its key themes.
To what extent does Rainbow’s End reveal both the emotional and intellectual responses provoked by the experience of discovering?
Harrison uses voice to encourage her audience to empathise with others outside their own context. Do you agree?
Rainbow’s End tells a tragic tale about the loss of culture and identity. Do you agree?
Examine the complexities of Dolly's character, exploring her struggles with identity and the impact of her cultural heritage on her relationships.
The struggle for justice is worth it, even if it takes a lifetime. Discuss the themes of justice and equality in Rainbow’s End and how the characters strive to overcome the barriers they face.
Rainbow’s End explores the instinctive human need to belong through relationships and social status. Discuss.
How does Harrison encourage readers to challenge their assumptions of the world through her depiction of individual human experiences?
In what ways does Harrison present the women living on The Flats as unsung heroes?
Gladys and Dolly are torn between the world of white Australians and their Indigenous Australian heritage. Discuss.
Despite the damaging history and continuing trying circumstances in Rainbow’s End, there is still hope. Discuss.
Rainbow’s End is pivotal in helping audiences come to new insights about belonging. To what extent do you agree?
“They forced us to leave … Our home.” Rainbow’s End explores the cruelty that humans can inflict on each other, no matter the time or place.
Rainbow’s End is a tribute to the enduring power of love and patience. How is this revealed in the play?
Harrison demonstrates that, although it is difficult, individual independence and familial duty can be balanced. Discuss in relation to Rainbow’s End.
Rainbow’s End explores discovery as a transformative journey for individuals. Discuss.
It is through storytelling that our ideas about the individual and collective human experience are challenged. Discuss.
Belonging is as much about choice as it is about circumstances that an individual cannot control. How does Harrison’s play explore this idea?
Rainbow’s End explores the life-altering discoveries of self, others, and the world. Discuss.
Discuss the stylistic choices made by Harrison to construct her play and to present its key themes.
Explore how voice within the play reflects the values and attitudes of the context within which it is set.
Rainbow’s End reveals that the values of individuals are shaped by their circumstances. Discuss.
Explore the significance of language and communication in the play, considering how it empowers or restricts the characters' experiences and relationships.
Harrison demonstrates that individual growth cannot occur if one does not challenge their pre-existing knowledge and understanding of their world. Do you agree?
Identify how Harrison uses language features to invite empathy for particular individuals in her play.
Analyse the connection between land, heritage, and cultural identity in Rainbow’s End, focusing on how the characters' relationships with the land shape their sense of self.
Rainbow’s End is a bitter condemnation of colonisation. Discuss.
How does Harrison lead audiences to question the assumptions and values within Australian society?
Analyse how language patterns are used to create meaning in the play.
Examine the concept of family in Rainbow’s End, exploring how it is defined and redefined by the characters throughout the play.
Change can be both scary and liberating. Discuss the theme of change in the play, considering the characters' fears, desires, and the potential for personal growth.
Discuss the development of Gladys in the play, exploring her transformation from a submissive housewife to a confident and independent woman.
Explore the role of dreams and aspirations in the play and how they impact the characters' choices and relationships.
The play suggests that characters have little control over their lives. Discuss.
It is the connection between storytelling and human experience that truly shapes an individual. To what extent does the play support this statement?
Society and perspectives may change over time, but the human condition remains constant. To what extent does Rainbow’s End explore this statement?
How does Harrison use language or structural innovations to unsettle her audience?
To what extent are Harrison’s characters affected by injustices of the past?
How does the play Rainbow’s End explore the experiences of displacement and assimilation for Aboriginal Australians?
“Just not going to happen. Not in my lifetime.” To what extent does Harrison demonstrate that change is possible?
Rainbow’s End demonstrates the importance of family and community in the lives of Indigenous Australians. Discuss.
Discuss the role of family in Rainbow’s End. How does family help the characters navigate their challenges, and how does it contribute to their sense of identity?
Analyse how different attitudes or perspectives are communicated through the construction of voices in Rainbow’s End.
Discuss the significance of the play's setting in shaping the experiences of the characters. How does the physical environment reflect their emotional states and their sense of belonging?
Rainbow’s End illustrates the racism and discrimination faced by generations of Aboriginal people in their own Australian society. Discuss.
“Why do we have to prove we can live like whitefellas, before we get the same opportunities?” How does Rainbow’s End explore injustice?
How does the play use language and symbolism to convey its themes? What techniques does the author use to engage the audience and create a sense of empathy for the characters?
Analyse the ending of the play. How does it resolve the conflicts that have been established, and what message does it leave with the audience?
Compare and contrast the experiences of different characters in Rainbow’s End. How do their backgrounds and personalities affect the way they respond to the challenges they face?
How does Rainbow’s End explore the theme of cultural heritage and the importance of maintaining cultural identity?
Cultural identity and heritage are key themes in Rainbow's End. Discuss their significance for the characters.
In her play, Jane Harrison exposes the effects of historical events on Indigenous Australians. Discuss.
Rainbow’s End is essentially a study of assimilation versus cultural preservation. To what extent do you agree with this evaluation of the play?
Analyse gender roles and expectations, as depicted in the play, and explore what views and values Harrison is endorsing through the attitudes and actions of her characters.
Harrison suggests that dreams and aspirations are especially important in circumstances when individuals are confronted with obstacles beyond their control. Discuss.
Explore the ways in which Rainbow’s End is a story about displacement and the search for belonging.
Harrison expounds the significance of education and knowledge in her play. How does she do this, and what are their effects on the characters?
Jane Harrison’s play illustrates that there are many catalysts which lead to an individual’s shift in perceptions. Discuss.
Harrison uses voice to encourage her audience to empathise with others outside their own context. Do you agree?
How does Harrison lead audiences to question the assumptions and values within Australian society?
Discuss the effectiveness of the ending of the play to position the audience to respond strongly to the themes or ideas presented by Harrison.
Jane Harrison’s play, Rainbow’s End, accentuates the correlation between individual and collective experiences. Discuss.
Analyse how language patterns are used to create meaning in the play.
“I’m not an interloper – I belong here – this is my land!” Analyse the tensions exposed by Jane Harrison in relation to the cultural conflicts experienced by various characters in Rainbow’s End.
Explore the shifting power dynamics among the characters. How do characters come to understand and appreciate more about both themselves and others, and what is the effect of these understandings?
‘The past is always present.’ Discuss how the past influences the characters' lives and decisions in Rainbow’s End.
Analyse the importance of friendship and cross-cultural relationships in Rainbow’s End, exploring how these connections challenge stereotypes and foster understanding.
Explore how Harrison has crafted the voices of her characters to reveal their inner or hidden conflicts.
The dream sequences in Rainbow’s End are the characters’ only escape from the depressing reality of life. Discuss.
To what extent does Rainbow’s End show that the characters’ optimism is justified?
“We demand the right to control our own destiny.” Rainbow’s End suggests that vulnerable people can control their own destinies. Do you agree?
How does Harrison represent the emotions arising from human experiences through the features of drama?
sb:
“Maybe that’s why it’s good, it’s true” Sunset Boulevard shows that
the truth, however ordinary or painful, is what matters. Do you agree?

In Billy Wilder’s Sunset Boulevard
things are never what they seem. Discuss.

Wilder’s Sunset Boulevard opposes
exploitation in all its forms. Discuss.

Sunset Boulevard explores different
kinds of madness. Discuss.

“There’s nothing tragic about being 50, not unless
you try to be 25”. Sunset boulevard is about the
need to face reality. Do you agree?

In Sunset Boulevard fantasy prevents the
truth from being confronted. To what extent do you agree?

"Sagittarius! I like Sagittarians - you can trust them”
Sunset Boulevard warns against trusting others. Discuss

“I felt trapped like the cigarette in the contraption
on her finger”. Sunset Boulevard is concerned with entrapment. Do you agree?

Sunset Boulevard explores the intermingling of
public and private realms. Discuss.

Billy Wilder’s Sunset Boulevard explores the
intersection of the written and the real. Discuss
Sunset Boulevard touches on the true horrors of Hollywood. Do you agree?
The structure of Sunset Boulevard is crucial to its message. Discuss
Sunset Boulevard is not just a sad tale of deception, greed, and jealousy. Discuss

I am big, it's the pictures that got small' Sunset Boulevard
shows that mistaken beliefs are responsible for suffering. Do you agree?

Sunset Boulevard argues that we have little control
over our lives. To what extent do you agree?

“But before you hear it all distorted and blown out of proportion,
before those Hollywood columnists get their hands on it, maybe you'd like to
hear the facts, the whole truth”. In Sunset Boulevard truth is the real victim. Discuss.
Sunset Boulevard reveals Hollywood as a world of predation and exploitation. Do you agree?

Sunset Boulevard explores the consequences of people
being in the wrong place or time. Discuss.

Billy Wilder’s Sunset Boulevard is a contest between
selfishness and self-sacrifice. Discuss.
In Sunset Boulevard, truth is not just distorted—it is dangerous. Do you agree?
Sunset Boulevard suggests that fame is both addictive and corrosive. Discuss.

Illusion offers comfort, but it always comes at a cost in

Sunset Boulevard. To what extent do you agree?

In Sunset Boulevard, the past is not a place of
nostalgia, but of entrapment. Discuss.
Sunset Boulevard warns us about the dangers of self-deception. Do you agree?
Power in Sunset Boulevard is rarely held—it is borrowed, stolen, or lost. Discuss.
In Sunset Boulevard, the pursuit of success often leads to personal ruin. Do you agree?
Suffering in Sunset Boulevard stems not from cruelty, but from longing. Discuss.

The film presents Hollywood not as a dream factory, but
as a place where dreams decay. Do you agree?
Sunset Boulevard shows that identity is unstable and often manufactured. Discuss.
Time in Sunset Boulevard is a cruel force that erodes relevance and worth. Do you agree?
In Sunset Boulevard, emotional survival requires denial. To what extent do you agree?
Sunset Boulevard explores the high price of reinvention. Discuss.
The film suggests that artifice is not just part of Hollywood—it is its foundation. Do you agree?
In Sunset Boulevard, silence speaks louder than truth. Discuss.

b.title: This is Me
b.s1:
‘Life isn’t about finding yourself.
Life is about creating yourself.’
b.s3:
My story is ready
There’s enough to share
As I have made plenty,
Come, sit at my table Listen and learn
And you will see me.

b.title: Finding My Way
b.s1:
‘… but it’s no use going back to yesterday,
because I was a different person then.’
b.s3:
‘The caterpillar transforms without thought.
Its journey to a beautiful butterfly, inevitable.
Why must my own metamorphosis
Be shaped by so many choices?’

b.title: Crossroads
b.s1: ‘Not all those who wander are lost.’
b.s3:
‘I walked a thousand miles.
Through shadows and through sun.
Each step a little louder
Until I found my run.’

b.title: Becoming
b.s1: ‘The only journey is the one within.’
b.s3:
‘A door closed.
Another creaked.
I wasn’t sure where I was going,
But I knew where I’d been.’

b.title: The Journey Within
b.s1:
‘Sometimes you find yourself in the middle of nowhere,
and sometimes, in the middle of nowhere, you find yourself.’
b.s3:
‘These shoes are worn,
Their soles thin and tired,
But they have taken me far
And they still carry dreams.’

b.title: A Fork in the Road
b.s1:
‘Two roads diverged in a wood,
and I—I took the one less travelled by.’
b.s3:
‘The map was blank
But my heart was full I drew the way
With trembling hand
And forged my fate.’

b.title: Unwritten Maps
b.s1:
‘It is good to have an end to journey toward;
but it is the journey that matters, in the end.’
b.s3:
‘I changed—not overnight
But slowly, fiercely
And when I looked back I saw a stranger
Smiling at me.

b.title: Metamorphosis
b.s1:
‘Growth is painful.
Change is painful.
But nothing is as painful as staying
stuck somewhere you don't belong.’
b.s3:
‘There is no path
Only steps we choose
Each one a story
Each one a truth.’

b.title: Turning Points
b.s1: ‘Every next level of your life will demand a different you.’
b.s3:
‘From silence I came 
Into my own voice
It shook at first 
Then sang.’

b.title: The Long Walk
b.s1:
‘You don’t have to see the whole staircase,
just take the first step.’
b.s3:
‘The road bent, then fell, then stood
Every bruise a lesson
Every scar a star.’

b.title: Paths Unknown
b.s1:
‘Sometimes the smallest step in the right direction
ends up being the biggest step of your life.’
b.s3:
‘I took a breath and stepped outside
Not sure where I’d go
But knowing I couldn’t stay’

b.title: Winds of Change
b.s1:
‘You cannot swim for new horizons
until you have courage to lose sight of the shore.’
b.s3:
‘It didn’t take a storm to move me
Just a whisper of wind
And a heart ready for change’

b.title: The Quiet Shift
b.s1: ‘The journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.’
b.s3:
‘They said it was too far
But they didn’t know I had nothing to lose
And everything to become’

b.title: Echoes of Tomorrow
b.s1:
‘There are far, far better things
ahead than any we leave behind.’
b.s3:
‘I dropped the map
And walked Into a world
Of what-ifs and why-nots’

b.title: Steps Unseen
b.s1:
‘Your life does not get better by chance,
it gets better by change.’
b.s3:
‘The platform was empty
But my hands were full
With dreams I hadn’t yet dared to name’

b.title: Edge of Becoming
b.s1: ‘Don't be afraid to give up the good to go for the great.’
b.s3:
‘A single step, shaky and unsure
Yet it changed everything
Even me’

b.title: Fragments of Me
b.s1:
‘If you’re always trying to be normal,
you will never know how amazing you can be.’
b.s3:
‘In the mirror, I saw pieces
Not broken, but waiting
To be arranged’

b.title: The Climb
b.s1:
‘What lies behind us and what lies before us
are tiny matters compared to what lies within us.’
b.s3:
‘The lantern didn’t show much
Just enough To keep going’

b.title: Wander and Wonder
b.s1:
‘To dare is to lose one’s footing momentarily. 
To not dare is to lose oneself.’
b.s3:
‘I followed the note
Not knowing who wrote it
But trusting the path it marked’

b.title: Reflections
b.s1:
‘Courage doesn’t always roar.
Sometimes courage is the quiet voice at the end of the day saying,
I will try again tomorrow.’
b.s3:
‘The wind howled behind me
But I faced forward
And jumped’

b.title: Paths We Take
b.s1: "You cannot discover new oceans unless you have the courage to lose sight of the shore."
b.s3:
The road ahead was uncertain,
but each step forward felt like a victory.'

b.title: Stepping Stones
b.s1:
"Success is not final, failure is not fatal:
it is the courage to continue that counts."
b.s3:
Each stone beneath my feet reminded me of
the times I’d slipped – but
also of every time I rose.'

b.title: The Other Side
b.s1:
"Growth begins where
comfort ends."
b.s3:
I looked back only once – then I stepped
into the unknown with quiet resolve.'

b.title: Against the Current
b.s1:
"You’ll never cross the ocean until you
have the courage to lose sight of the shore."
b.s3:
Pushing against the pull,
I found strength in resistance
and meaning in the struggle.'
